---
title: "Who wrote this? Scientific integrity at ETH Zurich in the age of AI"
author:
  - name: Lars Schöbitz
    affiliation: Global Health Engineering, ETH Zurich
    affiliation-url: https://ghe.ethz.ch
date: "2026-06-24"
abstract: |
  When an agent writes your code, drafts your prose, and compiles your
  literature, who is the author, and could you prove it? This post makes
  the case that scientific integrity in the age of AI is not a new
  standard but an old one, and shows how agentic coding, Git, and Quarto
  turn transparency into a side effect of how you work.
draft: true
---

ETH Zurich, and every other academic institution, has clear
guidelines for scientific integrity. The [ETH Zurich Guidelines on
Scientific Integrity](https://ethz.ch/en/research/ethics-and-animal-welfare/research-integrity.html)
(RSETHZ 414) rest on four basic principles: reliability, honesty,
respect, and responsibility (Art. 4). Reliability is explicitly
defined to include "transparency and traceability". The use of AI
tools does not make those principles blurry. It makes them harder to
honour.

When AI tools write the code to analyse the data, draft the prose
from a literature collection that AI compiled, and set up the methods
and tools for data collection, what is left that qualifies us
uniquely as scientists doing scientific work? And who, exactly, is
the author?

This is not a criticism, nor a call to stop using AI tools. Many of
us rely heavily on them and can no longer imagine working without
them. The bigger question is how we keep faith with scientific
integrity, and which policies we should establish for researchers to
declare their use of AI tools.

The guidelines already give us the test. An author must make "a
significant scientific contribution to the planning, execution,
control or evaluation of the research through personal work"
(Art. 14). Honorary authorship is "not permissible". The
contributions of individual authors "should be declared as
transparently as possible" (Art. 15). Sources must be "clearly
identifiable, comprehensible and traceable" (Art. 17). These rules
were written before a tool arrived that can do significant parts of
the work, cannot be listed as an author, and leaves no trace unless
you choose to keep one. The standard has not changed. The thing we
now have to be transparent and traceable about has.

In my daily practice, I have come to the conclusion that agentic
coding tools (Claude Code), combined with Git and GitHub and
open-source reproducible notebooks (Quarto), give me a way to
document exactly which parts of a project, be it data driven, prose
driven, or teaching material, are written by me and which are written
or assisted by an agentic tool.

In practice that means:

- Claude Code uses a `SKILL.md` to write every commit message. The message gives a detailed overview of all changes made to a set of files, and who made them.
- I decide whether the prompts used for the work in that commit are documented in a `prompts/` folder at the root of the repository.
- Claude Code uses a `SKILL.md` to open a pull request.
- I review the pull request, add comments, link in people, and merge once it is ready.

Every folder now carries a track record of the work done by a human
and by the machine. This is exactly the traceability that Art. 4 and
Art. 17 ask for, produced as a side effect of how I work rather than
as an afterthought. And the next time a journal asks for a statement
on the use of AI, you can point them to your public repository
instead of reconstructing the answer from memory.

As an example: this blog post was written entirely by a human first.
It was then added to a folder alongside a copy of the ETH guidelines.
Claude Code was used to open the folder, read the guidelines, and edit
the prose. The first draft is the [initial commit](https://github.com/Global-Health-Engineering/website/commit/0d259bdf79a515796cead43eef90b5a23edad9de), documented as my
contribution. The edits are [attributed to Claude](https://github.com/Global-Health-Engineering/website/commit/8b3099e42e03b597e2367ff573665c0d28f22b74). You can read the
whole history in the
[pull request](https://github.com/Global-Health-Engineering/website/pull/4),
and the exact prompts that produced these edits are archived,
verbatim, in the
[`prompts/` folder](https://github.com/Global-Health-Engineering/website/tree/main/prompts).

So, before your next manuscript, your next grant application, or your
next exam: who wrote it, and could you prove it?

## Want to try this yourself?

I run a two-part workshop series, *Agents for Scientists*, on this way
of working: Git, GitHub, and agentic coding for research, with a
traceable record of human and machine contributions from the first
commit to the final pull request. It is aimed at researchers who want
to use AI tools without losing sight of who did what. The two workshops are designed to be taken
together, with the first as a prerequisite for the second.

Sign up for both workshops at: <https://forms.gle/Zf5fPsPJqUxEoLgKA> 

Find out more at [github.com/agentsforsci](https://github.com/agentsforsci).

- **Workshop 1: Git and GitHub for Collaboration in Scientific Work.**
  15 July 2026, 09:30–14:30, at the
  [ETH Zurich CLD Building](https://ghe.ethz.ch/about/how-to-find-us.html).
  A hands-on introduction using the RStudio interface and the terminal
  with local installations. Details in the
  [workshop repository](https://github.com/gitforsci-ghe).
- **Workshop 2: Agentic Coding for Collaboration and Scientific Work.**
  September 2026, 08:30–17:00, at
  [Villa Hatt](https://ethz.ch/en/campus/access/region-zurich/villa-hatt.html),
  ETH Zurich. A critical, hands-on day on agentic coding tools:
  risk assessment, security concerns, handling of personal data, and
  treating agents as fully versioned and reviewable collaborators.

The series is funded by the [Swiss Reproducibility Network (SwissRN)](https://www.swissrn.org/contents/community/nodes/) local node at ETH Zurich & [Data Stewardship Network at ETH Zurich](https://library.ethz.ch/en/researching-and-publishing/data-management-and-policies/research-data-management/data-stewardship.html), funded by the [swissuniversities Open Science Programme](https://www.swissuniversities.ch/en/topics/open-science/open-science-programme) & [Global Health Engineering group at ETH Zurich](https://ghe.ethz.ch/)
